item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanna say I really enjoyed this article. I lurked on the site for a long time before I finally made an account, and it was even longer before I finally actually posted something. Back in the day, its honestly what sold me on the potential of the SCP wiki as a possible format for conveying ideas. Its not too complicated, not too complex, but the dilemma is still fun to think over. How it forces the Foundation to almost take a hard stance when they would be more than willing to sink other universes in most other cases is great imo.
Now all these years later, I've actually posted my first article, so I figured I would come back here and just say I enjoyed the read back in the day, and still enjoy it now. Its usually my go to article (and the exploring series video on it kek) to try to introduce people to SCP, or at least show what the format can do in a nice consumable bite. Who knows if I would be as into SCP if I didn't find this article lol

tl;dr good as fuck read, thanks for selling me on the idea of SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. I haven't felt chills like this since What Comes Next. Great job. It's always tricky when dealing with divergent timelines without making something either nonsensical or riddled with plot holes. But I suppose the great thing about the Foundation is that we're allowed to leave plenty up to the unknown. After all, the mystery is half the fun, isn't it?
As the 400th upvoter, I approve this message and this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'This' stands for the information (including the █████████████.███ document) which Isaac67 is about to give to BranchPrime.

'on this one' stands for 'in my (Isaac67's) universe'. He is saying that BranchPrime's O5's will likely censor the information Isaac is giving Branchprime, but since the universe is ending Isaac67's O5s won't have time to intercept his communications to BranchPrime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here is your regular reminder that this is the best piece of writing on the wiki, and if you haven't read it you're doing yourself a disservice.

Yes, you! You in recent posts! Read this immediately. This is the type of writing everyone on the wiki should aspire to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, in this scenario, means that they will not censor the phrase from BranchPrime

Is the [DATA EXPUNGED] the phrase you said? But it seems that there are no grounds for BranchPrime to say something different than the previous chat… So if the phrase could be said to be a breach of a protocol, wouldn't Isaac say that earlier in 1739-12?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe "this" refers to "what I'm about to tell you".

As the two are communicating from alternate timelines/realities, "on this end" I believe refers to the one the speaker is in. As in: "The O5s in your reality will cover this up, but the O5s in my reality are too busy to stop me from sending it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, in this scenario, means that they will not censor the phrase from BranchPrime

On this one, in this case, means that the O5's will not stop Issac with this case

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
User from the Korean branch. What does "this" and "on this one" stand for in this sentence below?

Isaac67: This is a breach of protocol, but it doesn't matter. In all likelihood the O5's going to censor this on your end but they're too busy to stop me on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a whole lot about Norse Mythology's Fenrir. Perhaps it is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Trying to re-rate this content?

It seems you have already rated this content and your current rating is: +1.”

Goddamn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since no one answered: They leashed god and sometimes make it kill their own sons because god demands it, and they complied to keep it distracted and satiated. God never calls it off and enthusiastically eats Isaac. That's it. The Foundation decided sacrificing Isaacs for their own survival was unethical and stopped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This method where branch isaacs can create even more isaacs… Doesn't this work like one of those spam mails? "if you dont wanna die, forward to more people"

If you create a universe, that universe knows it will die, and will be forced to create more isaacs to keep itself alive. It'd be a ridiculous infinite loop of exponentially increasing timelines, and… what if this is exactly what's needed to contain The gofetch Dog? Just what in hell are we dealing with here?

At least, they should make a few more timelines fight the gofetch Dog before terminating experiments to learn more about it, in case they need fight one themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dittoing these reasons for novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I not have this upvoted? +1'd with prejudice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speculation:

The people who created it have the ability to create divergent timelines by transferring matter back in time.

This thing appeared spontaneously.

It was probably sent from the future, creating a diverging timeline.

The question is, why?

I see two possibilities.

The first is that the original timeline was not able to fend off the dog, and before perishing created the split line by sending the laptop back in time, so as to provide the split line with a means of protecting itself.

The second is that the original timeline found a way to almost guarantee its survival. Create a divergent timeline, and then let that diverging timeline take the risk of using the program and creating further, sacrificial divergences.
yaaay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course, the best writer writes the best response for this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hammer the upvote button, for a single, simple reason.

Effective and mysterious use of "Does the black moon howl?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Umm… If you're referring to Genesis 22, God called that off at the last moment, and provided a stand-in.
Because of this, I'm not totally sure what parallel(s) you're trying to draw here. I may be hung up on the whole timeline-branch thing, or we may be working from different interpretations.
that said,
Could you please explain, or give a hint? I have a suspicion that it's something really nasty, and I sorta want to know.

As to the article itself: It's quite a creepy article, and it's done really well. The creep factor is even higher because you don't even realise how creepy it is until you get to the end.
Take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I posted over a year ago that I love this, and I still do! Here's why:

This is a well-executed example of a very cool idea. I agree it could be more show-y and less tell-y, but given how difficult making it show-y would be that's less of a flaw and more of an "area that could be better".

"gofetch.exe" is a really cool name, and I like the description/concept of the animation. Something about it has stuck in my mind for the entire year- the idea of an old, pixelated, few-frames-a-minute animation that represents something so enormous and indescribable is a great bit.

The slow bring-out of information is very well handled and paced, drawing things out without dragging, and dangling enough to keep the reader interested. Also, in general I'm just a sucker for cosmic, so-far-beyond-our-comprehension-even-describing-the-distance-is-impossible horror.

The best part though are the little existential conversations, and the characterization of the researcher. Through nothing but snippets, we get an excellent picture of this researcher is- one cold, self-serving, borderline-sociopathic, but still with enough inner conflict to ground him in humanity. It's a level almost never seen in scips (I'd even say it's the best example), and even in tales it can be difficult to find. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? Seemed quite clear to me. The again, I'm 2 years down the line, maybe there have been edits since :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OH CRAP! It makes sense!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're always named Isaac. They come from a single universe; it could be said that the single universe that lives is their father.

So ask yourself, where else has someone named Isaac been offered up by his father to a power that can't be contended with? What's really happening here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantasic. Seriously, this could be an Apollyon-class SCP, but you present it in a charmingly ambiguous way.

Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this.

Question: by stopping testing, wouldn't we just be dooming our own universe sooner?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad I linked this in chat to show more people, but this is slowly sneaking into one of my favorite skips the more I reflect and think on it. Even with the exposition dump at the end, it does it in a way that doesn't feel tacky or forced, and the implications and moral/ethical dillemas of dooming universes to buy our universe a little more time is incredible.

The most mind-bending and interesting part about this is one that I overlooked the first few times I read this. Why are we allowed to talk to the people who's universe we doomed if they're spelling out what's happening? There's definitely more to this than it lets on, and it's intentional. *shudders*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, this is amazing. My favorite type of horror is existential horror, where the validity of existence itself is thrown into question; combine that with a healthy dose of malevolent Eldritch entity and you've got an easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real mindfuck with this one is that everything in this, the universes ending, is partially speculation; we're not sure whether or not this really is destroying universes, let alone if we really are communicating with alternate universes in the first place.

I'm giving this one of my golden upvotes, for multiple reasons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm skeptical, but it works. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely agree with this sentiment. However, I think that it would take a ridiculous amount of skill to show rather than tell all of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the mind-screw of this. IF we use it, we run the risk of being the new branch. If we DON'T use it, there is every chance the "dog" will get strong enough to break containment and eat OUR timeline. Dankaar nailed it in one. The dog wins.

I'm going to keep chewing on this one for awhile. There's just so much possibility here.

+1 either way. I personally like the "mundane" lead-in. Makes the anvil over your head less obvious until you finally notice it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So lemme get this straight; everyone is in the same universe, until this is used, which causes a branch in the timeline. That branch is then destroyed. So, the problem is that we're in the universe that doesn't diverge yet, so we may branch off and be destroyed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, Morton's Fork delimma.

The dog likes eating universes. So it makes SCP-1739, and sends it to the Foundations.
#1. If the Foundation uses it, it gets to eat a universe and run around for a bit.
#2. If the Foundation doesn't use it, it gets to eat a universe, and may or may not stay chained up.

Either way, the dog wins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Laptops aren't naturally occurring. Someone made this device, which is capable of creating alternate timelines, in order to satiate or contain this Boogum. So whoever made this device, to make alternate timelines, has the capacity and power to make alternate timelines.

There HAS to be a more efficient way to use that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've made some changes to this article to better fit the changing standards of this site. I've also added a CC-compliant image: source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Food for thought, which very well make no sense…

We're assuming that this creature is kept sated by the offerings. What if it isn't? Not completely, at any rate—part of it is, maybe the larger part, but part of it isn't.

What if it wants out? Maybe that's why it can communicate with doomed universes, maybe that's why it ended up in the hands of the Foundation—exactly so they could figure out what was going on, then watch as the Foundation stopped using it… resulting it in getting stronger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple reasons (Anaxagoras, I think, was also asking this question):

There's a reason why the O5 is keeping this thing on-hand. Like, if we really, really, wanted this thing out of sight and mind, we could probably do it? It's not a 682. I mean, even if launching into the sun won't destroy it, people aren't going to find it. The point of Operation Smokescreen isn't to destroy this thing, it's to make sure that we aren't the bait, in the event that we need to start throwing out balls. And we'll know if we ever need to start throwing out balls, because research has given us what the warning signs of a restless dog will be.

…and even then, it's ethically ambiguous whether or not it's morally justified to continually sacrifice multiple universes to save one.

So that's why we're not sending people into the past. But there's also another thing…

+ show block
– hide block

…if the person who made this thing wanted a continual source of balls, then why did they "give" this machine to the Foundation? It spontaneously appeared in containment right? And then, why would they give the machine a chat client to communicate with the doomed universes? What's the point? It'll just give the Foundation an opportunity to figure out what's going on, won't it?

The implication being that there are nuances to the threat that the Foundation doesn't entirely realize. (i am not entirely sure what they are either, but i have a vague idea)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: we risk destruction then don't we? How can it be contained without that risk?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoilers
- hide

The person created SCP-1739; their goal is to keep the "mad dog" contained and that particular timeline safe. Each time you send someone back in time, this creates a branch timeline that is used to keep containment of the dog. If you send somebody back into the past, you're posed with a Prestige-type dilemma—you don't know if you're "going to be" the branch timeline or not. That is, if you say, "okay, at Y we send somebody back in time to X," X being in the past relative to Y…you don't know, at X, if somebody will show up or not. There will be a timeline where somebody does not show up at X, and that timeline is safe. But you don't know if you're in it or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What exactly does he mean by

"Isaac132: Somewhere there is a person living in a timeline where SCP-1739 never deposits a traveler from the future. This person very much does not want the mad dog to grow too restless. Your Foundation and your O5 Council should hope that they are existing in this timeline."

Who are they referring to, and why does it matter that they are sending people into the past?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a little "tell-y", as a previous poster noted, but beyond that minor handicap I quite like the story, and I like it better as I reread it. The conversations between Isaac and BranchPrime are more subtle than I first realized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of agree with Mulciber and you, but on the other hand I kind of don't. While yes, it would probably be a good thing story-wise to have more foreshadowing, there are two reasons why I wouldn't want to put obvious hints. The first is that this is, in-universe, a technical document, not an actual story. Second is that I feel I already have some subtle foreshadowing—why is this thing Keter? What the hell's the animation?

As to your suggestions, that seems like it would just lengthen the article without really contributing any narrative or story. I mean, they're not bad suggestions, I just personally have problems with article bloat and I try avoiding it when I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree whole-heartedly. I almost checked out mentally until I got through the standard article fields as well. Perhaps the article could be improved with a touch more foreshadowing as to the anomalous nature of the laptop. I'm an engineering student (so maybe this is just me) but I couldn't help but wonder about some of the more technical aspects. The article references an unknown chat protocol… That got me thinking about some of the other tests the Foundation might run on the machine. Have they tried to analyze how it's communicating exactly? What happens if they attempt to copy "gofetch.exe" to another laptop and run it there? Or de-compile the executable?

The answers to these questions may just open up more questions, but they seem like things the Foundation may try to attempt. On the other hand they may simply take away from the article. Take it as food for thought.

Regardless, I really like this SCP. Upvote from me =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I wouldn't say it was obvious, but once I realized it, it was clear that's what you were doing, and it made the dialogue instantly go from "a little odd" to "really intimate" in a weird way makes complete sense if think about how weird and intimate it would be to have a conversation with yourself. Very well done dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the concept and find it darkly clever, but I do wish it were more evident what was going on sooner. It was challenging to get through the standard article fields with my interest intact. It isn't until the chatlog that the core concept is revealed. Seems like with an idea this intriguing, we could see it a bit earlier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice execution of a great concept. Awesome job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for this type of time-travel stories, but even if I wasn't, this is so well done that it would convert me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was exposition heavy but I didn't care. The concept was good enough to make explicit explanation worth every word.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that the Isaacs are the same person as the researcher, along with the subtle hints to his own prior existential struggles.

well gold star for you :p. nobody i've asked has gotten that first time around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Wow. Upvoted hard. So many good things.

-You've effectively contained a universe-ending horror with a Foundation-verse plausible method. Well done.

-This starts off as a stupid corporate laptop computer and escalates into universe-ending Keter horror. Great flow.

-You've effectively captured the existential horror of being a tiny spec on some cosmic horror's tuna fish sandwich in a skip.

-I love that the Isaacs are the same person as the researcher, along with the subtle hints to his own prior existential struggles.

-Not sure what's worse… Using this over and over again to contain Dog, or NOT using it and letting him get stronger. Interesting choice of ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spooky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I definitely see your point here. The Isaac/Branch conversation used to be less direct, but this ran the risk of making the article simply incomprehensible. I really don't know how'd I make this more tell-y while simultaneously explaining everything clearly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The woman throws another ball and then the dog comes back with two. There's a ton of itty-bitty mechanics that don't really matter that I guess I could have included in a test log that wouldn't have mattered so I didn't write it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if you use the device while the dog is already out?

I agree with Famine, but I think the concept is cool enough that I'm upvoting regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might be talkin' out of my ass here, but I dislike how the narrative does more tell than show. I like the concept and I like what they're explaining.

It would be more palatable to see it laid out in a format other than "here is the concept and here is why horror" verbatim. So I guess my major qualms with this would be the flow and the narrative device itself. Branch seems like a sloppy reader surrogate to me.
I find this beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What you told me in chat was there was one Isaac, but you didn't really mention different Issac, but this cleared it up.
